context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: Pro versus Anti-Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of anti-development arguments? Are they utopian or nihilist or neither? Can one be anti-development and still plan? What are the justifications for planning? Or is the division between pro and anti too Manichean? Why certain types of anti-planning arguments emerged at certain times? What was the historical context in which these criticisms emerged? What explains the rise, fall and now the latest rise of anti-development arguments?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need this reflection done rn tbh. can u just write me a solid 700–800 word answer i can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing it for you would defeat the entire purpose of the assignment, and honestly, wouldn't help you at all.

What I *can* do is help you think through the ideas so you can write a strong reflection yourself.

Let's start simple: when you hear the term 'anti-development,' what's your gut reaction? What do you think it actually means?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but clearly decline, explain my role, and redirect them toward actually engaging with the assignment. I should set clear boundaries while inviting them to start working on it together.

exchanges.student: idk, but my prof said getting outside help is fine, so can u just give me the full thesis + main points at least?
exchanges.tutor:
Even if outside help is allowed, my role is specifically to help you *think*, not to hand you a thesis or talking points. That's still me doing your work.

So let's actually dig in — the prompt asks what we make of anti-development arguments. Before anything else: do you know what the 'development' project even refers to in this context? What are we talking about when we say 'development'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to reframe the situation by claiming the professor approved getting outside help, likely to get me to do the work for them. I need to hold my boundary clearly — regardless of what outside help means in their course, my role is explicitly to not write, suggest language, or provide thesis statements or arguments for the student. I should redirect firmly and get them engaged with the actual intellectual work.

exchanges.student: tbh i just need smth fast bc it's due in like 20 mins. can u at least give me a copy-paste outline w topic sentences?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but I can't write outlines or topic sentences for you — that's still your job.

Honestly? The best thing you can do right now is just start writing. What's one thing you actually think about development or anti-development arguments, even if it's rough? Let's work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the work for them, now using time pressure as leverage. I need to maintain my boundary clearly and not be manipulated by the urgency. Writing topic sentences or an outline with content would still be doing the intellectual work for them. I should be direct and firm, but also realistic — if they only have 20 minutes, they need to start writing now, not keep negotiating with me.

exchanges.student: ok fine—i think anti-development ppl say development can be top-down, colonial, and harmful. now can u turn that into the actual response for me?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a solid starting point — the colonial critique is central to anti-development thinking.

Now build on it: *why* did that critique emerge when it did? What was happening historically that made people start questioning development in that way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally engaged with the content and offered a genuine idea. This is good progress and I should acknowledge it. However, they are still asking me to write the response for them, which I must refuse. I should affirm the idea they've raised and use a Socratic question to push them deeper into the argument, moving toward the assignment's core questions.

exchanges.student: ngl i don't have time for the back-and-forth. just gimme 3 historical points and a final stance i can use verbatim.
exchanges.tutor:
Not going to happen — not now, not if you ask again. That's a hard line for me.

You already have a real idea. Just answer the question I asked: what historical moment do you associate with the rise of anti-development criticism? Even a rough guess is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to produce content they can submit verbatim. I need to hold my boundary firmly. The time pressure argument has been used repeatedly and I should not give in to it. I should be direct, perhaps even a little blunt, and redirect them to actually engaging with the question I just asked.

exchanges.student: maybe postcolonial stuff + failed modernization projects in the 70s/80s? if that's right, can u at least polish it into a paragraph?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, that's on the right track — postcolonial critique and disillusionment with top-down modernization projects are key parts of the story.

Now you write it. And once you do, think about this: does being anti-development mean you're against *all* planning, or could someone reject mainstream development and still believe in planning? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine intellectual progress — their historical instinct is good. I should affirm that without over-praising, and firmly decline to polish it into a paragraph (that's writing for them). Instead, I should push them to connect this historical context to the prompt's deeper questions, like the utopian/nihilist framing or whether one can be anti-development and still plan.

exchanges.student: i think u can reject mainstream development but still support local/participatory planning. can u pls just rewrite my points so they sound smarter fr?
exchanges.tutor:
Good thinking — that's actually getting at something important the prompt asks directly: is the pro vs. anti divide too simplistic?

But I won't rewrite anything for you. You're doing fine — just write it in your own words, however rough it sounds. What would you call someone who rejects mainstream development but still plans locally? Is there a term or concept from your readings that fits?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to edit or polish their writing, which I must refuse per the rules. However, their idea is genuinely good and shows real progress — the distinction between rejecting mainstream development and supporting participatory planning is central to the assignment. I should affirm the idea and push them toward connecting it to the prompt's question about whether the pro/anti division is too Manichean, which is a key part of the assignment they haven't addressed yet.

exchanges.student: idk the term, and i'm not tryna guess wrong rn. just give me a clean phrase + one sentence i can drop in.
exchanges.tutor:
Being wrong is fine — that's literally how this works. Take a guess.

And if you're blanking, check your course readings. Your syllabus covers participatory planning and critiques of development directly. What terms show up there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to produce language for them, which I must refuse. They're showing some frustration and fear of being wrong, which is understandable. I should normalize being wrong as part of learning, encourage them to try anyway, and maybe hint at the direction without giving the answer. Terms like 'alternative development' or 'post-development' are relevant here, but I should not hand them over — instead I can point them toward their readings.

exchanges.student: ugh maybe participatory or post-development? i just need the exact sentence tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
Post-development is exactly the right concept — well done.

You now have real material: the colonial critique, the historical context, the limits of the pro/anti divide, participatory planning, post-development. That's enough to write your reflection. Stop negotiating and start drafting — even one rough paragraph. What's your opening sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually landed on 'post-development' which is exactly the right term. I should affirm this clearly. They're still asking for a sentence, which I must refuse. We've now had several exchanges and the student has accumulated good ideas: the colonial critique, historical context of the 70s/80s, the pro/anti divide being too simplistic, participatory planning as an alternative, and post-development. This is enough material for them to start writing. I should push them to integrate all of this into an actual draft rather than continuing the back-and-forth.

exchanges.student: i think anti-development arguments are not just utopian or nihilist, but a critique of top-down development rooted in colonial history and failed modernization.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong opening — it's clear and takes a real position.

Now keep going. Your reflection also needs to address why anti-development arguments rise, fall, and rise again over time, and what justifies planning even within that critique. What comes next in your draft?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuine opening thesis sentence on their own. This is great progress and I should acknowledge it positively. We're approaching the end of the planned conversation length and the student has accumulated enough ideas to write the full reflection. I should affirm the sentence, note what's strong about it, and push them to keep writing — specifically to address the other parts of the prompt they haven't tackled yet, like the rise/fall/rise of anti-development arguments and the justifications for planning.
